title: Making a simple book press from scrap timber
subtitle: Two pieces of board and four bolts, enough pressure for home bookbinding
excerpt: A book press applies even, sustained pressure across the full face of a text block while adhesive dries. The simplest version is two flat boards and four wing-nut bolts. It takes an hour to make and solves the warped-board problem permanently.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on bookbinding tools: the nipping press and the lying press are described in detail. The bolt-and-board version described here is the home-made equivalent of the nipping press, documented in 20th-century craft bookbinding literature as a low-cost substitute.

glossaryTerms.definition: A screw press used in bookbinding to apply downward pressure to a text block or case immediately after gluing.

content.text: , a cast-iron or wooden screw press weighing 15-30 kg. The home equivalent is two flat boards, four threaded bolts through corner holes, and four wing nuts. It won't produce as much pressure as a nipping press, but it holds a text block flat under sustained load, which is all that's needed.

items.name: Plywood or MDF, 18 mm thick, two pieces A3 size
items.notes: Flat and unwarped. Check by laying each piece on a flat surface, it should not rock.

items.name: M8 coach bolts, four, 100-120 mm long
M8 washers, eight
items.notes: One under each bolt head, one under each wing nut, to prevent the bolt pulling through.

content.text: Mark and drill the bolt holes

Stack the two boards precisely on top of each other and clamp them together. Mark the four hole positions 30 mm from each corner. Drill through both boards at once, this guarantees the holes align. A 9 mm hole for an M8 bolt gives just enough clearance for the bolt to slide freely.

Thread a washer and bolt through the bottom board at each corner, bolt head facing down. Rest the book or test block on the lower board, lay the upper board over it, thread on the remaining washers and wing nuts. Tighten the wing nuts by hand in a diagonal sequence, front-left, then back-right, then front-right, then back-left, to apply pressure evenly rather than tilting the upper board.

Always place waxed paper or polyethylene sheet between the press boards and the book so that any squeezed-out PVA doesn't glue the press boards to the cover. Check the wing nuts after five minutes, PVA creep allows the boards to compress further and the pressure can be increased. Leave in the press until fully dry: at least two hours for PVA, longer if the workshop is cold.
